data.title: Apple rises after 'solid' Q3, as Wedbush says iPhone China-beat 'star of the show'
data.description:
Wedbush said solid results from Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), with an impressive iPhone beat in China, was the star of the show, following the U.S. tech giant's quarterly results post-market on Thursday.

The firm maintained its Outperform rating on Apple with a $270 price target on the stock. Shares of Apple rose about 2% premarket on Friday.

"Apple announced its FY3Q25 ((June)) results which came in much better than expected featuring strong top and bottom-lines led by iPhone and Service particularly in China which return to positive growth (up 4% y/y) which is a key theme for Apple as this core region has been a persistent growth headwind over the past year," said analysts led by Daniel Ives.

The analysts noted that Apple reported total revenues of $94.04B which came in well ahead of the Street's estimate of $89.35B estimate with double-digit growth across iPhone, Mac and Services led by the all-important iPhone revenues of $44.58B which was well above the Street’s expectation of $40.29B as  the company saw significant improvements in the key China region heading intothe iPhone 17 product cycle over the next year.

"This was a major step in the right direction for Cook and Cupertino with China the star of the show," said the analysts.

Ives and his team said that China, importantly, delivered $15.37B in revenue, (up 4% year-over-year) as the key region was considerably better than both Wedbush and the Street’s expectations by returning to positive growth as government stimulus helped stimulate demand during the quarter.

data.title: Stock index futures trade lower as Trump's tariffs weigh; investors await jobs data
data.description:
Stock index futures traded in the red on Friday, as President Donald Trump issued revised tariff orders, while investors awaited a key jobs report. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -0.7%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -0.8%, and Dow futures (INDU) -0.7%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.39%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.96%.

Trump on Thursday signed an executive order revising “reciprocal” tariffs on dozens of countries, setting new duty rates between 10% and 41%. According to the White House, any goods deemed to be transshipped to evade these duties will incur an additional 40% tariff.

Wall Street on Thursday limped to a negative finish as a post-earnings surge in tech titans Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) was overshadowed by jitters over tariffs and economic data that showed stubborn inflation and weak personal spending.

"Equity markets have seen contrasting themes over the last 24 hours, as a resurgence of U.S. 'tech-ceptionalism' was offset by a broader loss of momentum,” Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

On the earnings side, Amazon (AMZN) was down 8% in premarket trade as its Q3 outlook disappointed. Apple (AAPL) was up 1.5% as it beat its third-quarter estimates, helped by higher-than-expected iPhone sales.

On the economic side, the July nonfarm payrolls report will come in before the opening bell. Payrolls are expected to be up 110K in July, down from 147K in June as hiring tapers off. The jobless rate is expected to tick up to 4.2 percent from 4.1 percent in June.

Staying with data, today brings the latest payrolls number. Our U.S. economists expect headline payrolls to slow to +75k in July (from +147K in June) amid payback from a likely seasonal June spike in education employment, and private payrolls to gain +100k (previously +74K in June), with the unemployment rate edging up to 4.2%, Reid added. 

"Today’s employment report is important—rate cuts are likely to be contingent on labor market weakness. Policy uncertainty (around trade taxes and other policies) means that firms have been reluctant to hire," UBS' Paul Donovan said. 

The problem is that labor market data is less reliable than it used to be, and a rogue number may change the willingness of a “data dependent” Fed to change rates, Donovan added. 

Other economic releases for the day include the July PMI manufacturing final and the July ISM manufacturing index. Both are expected to come in at 49.5.

The July consumer sentiment report will land during market hours.

data.title: Hanover signals 6%-7% net written premium growth for H2 2025, supported by strong Q2 performance and tech investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Hanover Insurance Group (THG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John Conner Roche, President and CEO, described the quarter as a period of "extraordinary progress," highlighting "a record" operating ROE of 18.7% and operating earnings of $4.35 per diluted share, with earnings growth of approximately 25% on an ex-CAT basis. He emphasized the company's "differentiated approach" through select distribution and strong agent relationships, stating, "Our business outlook remains very positive. With widespread profitability and target level returns across most segments, we're well positioned to capitalize on emerging opportunities and to continue delivering high-quality results going forward."
  • Roche underlined top line acceleration, noting "top line growth started to accelerate in the second quarter, and we expect that to strengthen as we progress through the second half of the year and into 2026."
  • Specialty segment performance was noted, with "upper single digit to double-digit growth across E&S, which grew 22%, surety up 13% and health care, which increased 8%."
  • Strategic investments in AI and workflow automation were highlighted: "We're making deliberate strategic investments that are fueling smarter, more scalable growth...we are also leveraging generative AI and AI capabilities to automate account submission ingestion, build triage functionality and streamline workflow automation."
  • Jeffrey Mark Farber, CFO, stated, "In the second quarter, we delivered a combined ratio of 92.5%, an improvement of 6.7 points year-over-year. Excluding catastrophes, our combined ratio of 85.5% improved 3 points compared to last year's second quarter."

Outlook

  • Management projected net written premium growth of "6% to 7% range for the second half of 2025."
  • Farber stated, "Our third quarter cat load is expected to be 6.8%."
  • The company expects "growth of net written premiums to be in the 6% to 7% range for the second half of 2025."
  • Management indicated continued strong pricing in Personal Lines and Specialty, stating, "We expect overall pricing to remain strong through the remainder of the year."

Financial Results

  • The company reported operating earnings of $4.35 per diluted share and operating ROE of 18.7%.
  • Net investment income increased 16.7% to $105.5 million.
  • Combined ratio for the quarter was 92.5%, with an ex-CAT combined ratio of 85.5%.
  • Book value increased 6% sequentially and 13.2% year-to-date.
  • Approximately 170,000 shares were repurchased during Q2 for $27.6 million, with $244 million remaining under the repurchase program.
  • Personal Lines growth was 3.7%, Specialty net written premium growth was 4.6%, and Core Commercial net written premiums grew 4.4%.
  • Notable improvements in combined ratios and loss ratios were reported across business segments.

Q&A

  • Michael Wayne Phillips, Oppenheimer: Asked about rate environment and portfolio mix in Specialty. Bryan James Salvatore responded that pricing "is still resilient. It's still consistent with loss trend...we have increasingly differentiated ourselves in the ability to write that lower middle market and the small business where there is less volatility and the pricing can remain more resilient."
  • Phillips inquired about commercial auto charge trends. CFO Farber answered, "The commercial auto is a relatively small line...we've got a fairly small risk profile and the increase in reserves is IBNR. In fact, our case reserve levels are down and IBNR is up significantly."
  • Phillips also questioned bodily injury severity in personal auto. Farber replied, "Largely just prudence there. With the frequency benefit that we're seeing, it's just appropriate what we're hearing in the market and worrying about liability and lawyer involvement."
  • Michael David Zaremski, BMO: Queried about catastrophe load guidance. Farber responded, "6.8% is the number that we gave for the third quarter...personal lines will see some improvement, but also commercial lines in terms of thinning the larger PML items of cat risk."
  • Zaremski asked about pricing competition in Commercial Lines and Small Commercial. Roche stated, "Our resiliency comes from where we play...our small account focus where the pricing pressures probably they don't show up as much as they do in the higher-end property spaces."
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Sought details on Personal Lines distribution expansion. Roche replied, "We're comfortable with our geographic footprint as it is today...what we are doing, though, is further diversifying our business across the PL footprint that we have."
  • Newsome also asked how to observe tech impact as an outsider. Richard William Lavey, COO, explained, "We absolutely are targeting our efforts on our transformation work, thinking about it as a growth enabler really to help us scale the company."
  • Meyer Shields, KBW: Asked about reserve adjustments and accident year picks. Farber confirmed, "We did increase our loss pick in commercial auto in core commercial in the current year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' questions were neutral to slightly positive, focusing on sustainability of favorable trends, competitive environment, and growth drivers. There was interest in how technology investments and risk management strategies translate into results but limited skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, supported by statements like "We couldn't be more excited about our prospects moving forward" (Roche) and "We are very pleased with our outstanding results in the second quarter" (Farber). The tone was consistent with the previous quarter, with increased emphasis on momentum and growth.
  • Compared to Q1, management appeared more assertive about accelerating growth and technological advancement, while analysts remained focused on underlying drivers with minimal pushback.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to explicit targets for accelerated premium growth in H2 2025.
  • Strategic focus expanded to emphasize technology-driven efficiency and AI integration in Q2, building on operational discipline and pricing agility previously highlighted.
  • Analysts' focus moved from competition and pricing pressure in Small Commercial (Q1) to sustainability of segment growth and technology impact in Q2.
  • Key metrics such as operating ROE, combined ratio, and net investment income showed sequential improvement. Share repurchase activity and capital management remained robust.
  • Management confidence increased, with more concrete projections and a greater emphasis on leveraging technology for future growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing vigilance regarding tariff impacts, with Roche stating, "we have not yet seen a material impact, we do anticipate some minor loss cost increases emerging in the back half of the year."
  • Catastrophe risk remains a concern, with Farber noting "Despite a relatively active quarter for severe convective storms, catastrophe losses of 7 points came in below our second quarter assumption, highlighting the effectiveness of our catastrophe management actions."
  • Liability and commercial auto trends are being closely monitored, with conservative reserving and loss pick adjustments in response to observed severity and litigation activity.
  • No significant analyst concerns were deflected; management addressed questions directly and emphasized preparedness and adaptability.

Final Takeaway

The Hanover Insurance Group delivered a record second quarter, highlighted by robust operating earnings, strengthening top line growth, and meaningful improvements in profitability metrics across all segments. Management projects net written premium growth of 6% to 7% for the second half of 2025, supported by the company’s disciplined underwriting, targeted pricing strategies, and increasing technology investment, particularly in AI and workflow automation. With a resilient and diversified portfolio, continued expense management, and a focus on expanding growth opportunities, the company enters the second half positioned to maintain strong returns and capitalize on evolving market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: European markets in red as new Trump tariffs trigger wave of risk-off sentiment
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.66%

Germany (DAX:IND) -1.51%

France (CAC:IND) -1.57% 

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) fell over 1% on Friday following the Trump administration's tariff rejig, with European pharmaceutical stocks falling after the President sent letters demanding cheaper drug prices.

Frankfurt's DAX fell more than 1% to near 23,700 on the first trading day of August, its lowest since July 1.

Trump’s executive order imposed tariffs ranging from 10% to 41% on dozens of countries, and subjected all goods considered to have been transshipped to an additional 40% duty.

On the economic front, HCOB Eurozone manufacturing PMI hit a 36-month high, at 49.8 in July, while Germany's MI was revised slightly lower to 49.1 in July, continuing to point to a deterioration in manufacturing business conditions.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 3 basis points at 4.49%.

Germany's 10-year yield was unchanged at 2.72%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 7 basis points at 4.64%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Skyward targets profitable growth in agriculture, credit, and A&H amid selective cycle management
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Skyward Specialty Insurance Group (SKWD) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Scott Robinson reported "another outstanding quarter with adjusted operating income of $37.1 million or $0.89 per diluted share, driven by $31.2 million of pretax underwriting income, our best in company history." He highlighted an annualized return on equity of 19.1%, gross written premiums growth of 18% for the quarter, and a company-best combined ratio of 89.4%. Robinson emphasized the company's ability to "generate profitable growth in areas less exposed to the cycles impacting the broader P&C market" and noted a deliberate pullback in global and E&S property due to softening conditions while holding liability exposure flat. He stated, "Our growth in areas, including ag, credit and A&H demonstrates, again, that we are exceptionally well positioned to adapt and reallocate capital elsewhere in our portfolio to continue to grow underwriting income."
  • CFO Mark William Haushill said, "We had another strong quarter, reporting adjusted operating income of $37.1 million or $0.89 per diluted share and net income of $38.8 million or $0.93 per diluted share." He added that agriculture, credit, accident and health, captives, and specialty programs contributed meaningfully to growth and highlighted a decline in investment income to $18.6 million due to alternative asset portfolio volatility. Haushill noted, "Excluding alternative investments, net investment income increased 23.5% over the prior year due to the 30% increase in income from our fixed income portfolio, driven by a higher portfolio yield and a significant increase in the invested asset base."
  • Robinson discussed strategic moves in technology, stating, "We believe that we are in a leading position using AI in this regard, particularly in the specialty insurance markets where we compete."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a disciplined approach to growth, maintaining selectivity in casualty exposure due to concerns about loss inflation. The company noted a continued positive rate environment but refrained from increasing liability exposure, reflecting a cautious market posture.
  • Haushill reaffirmed, "We have ample debt financing flexibility," and indicated expectations for continued operational efficiency with an expense ratio "in line with our expectations of sub 30s."
  • Robinson confirmed ongoing commitment to the "Rule Our Niche" strategy, with an emphasis on adapting to market conditions and reallocating capital to high-return areas such as agriculture, credit, and A&H.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted operating income was $37.1 million, net income $38.8 million, and gross written premiums grew by 18%. Net written premiums grew by 14%, and net retention through six months was 60.9%. The combined ratio was 89.4%, including 1.4 points of cat losses. The non-cat loss ratio of 59.9% improved 0.7 points compared to 2024. There was no net reserve development this quarter, and IBNR made up more than 70% of net reserves.
  • The expense ratio of 28.1% improved 0.9 points year over year, and operating income benefited from underwriting results, though investment income was impacted by alternative asset volatility. $30 million of capital was returned from the alternative asset portfolio and reinvested in fixed income, and embedded yield was 5.3% at June 30. Financial leverage was just under 12% debt-to-capital.

Q&A

  • Charles Gregory Peters, Raymond James: Asked about reserving in growth lines like ag and credit. Robinson responded, "We take a conservative position. I think that our bias towards lines that we believe have volatility will take even a more conservative position in the range."
  • Peters inquired about alternative investments. Robinson stated, "Am I happy with the volatility in this quarter? No, I'm not happy with the volatility in this quarter, but that's the way business is sometimes."
  • Michael David Zaremski, BMO: Queried on MGA alignment. Robinson explained, "15% of our premium falls into the delegated authority, program administrator MGA kind of market, but those relationships are deeply strategic. They're not transactional."
  • Taylor Alexander Scott, Barclays: Asked about captive premium growth and A&H exposure. Robinson highlighted the success of a property-focused captive in the automotive dealer market and described unique cost management approaches in A&H, noting, "We are negotiating the payment before we actually pay."
  • Meyer Shields, KBW: Questioned surety loss activity and commercial auto reserves. Robinson said, "Surety is -- it's been unbelievable to be honest," and Haushill clarified, "Loss picks are not changing."
  • Andrew E. Andersen, Jefferies: Asked about the amended 10-K filing and session rate. Haushill described the amendment as administrative and explained retention dynamics linked to captives and business mix.
  • Matthew John Carletti, Citizens: Inquired about the new aviation unit. Robinson outlined the unit's focus on personal aircraft and plans for growth.
  • Andrew Scott Kligerman, TD Cowen: Asked about retention and casualty growth focus. Robinson provided a detailed explanation of retention dynamics and described selective casualty exposure growth.
  • Mark Douglas Hughes, Truist: Sought guidance on cat loss assumptions. Haushill maintained guidance of roughly 2 points for the full year and confirmed no changes to loss picks.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pressed on reserve conservatism, investment volatility, and growth area sustainability, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with a focus on risk management and execution.
  • Management tone was confident and disciplined during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing conservative reserving, selective growth, and technological differentiation. Robinson's language signaled confidence: "We are exceptionally well positioned" and "We have every business seeking to leverage the powerful advancements we have been implementing."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a steady, constructive tone, with little evidence of increased skepticism or defensiveness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, stressing disciplined cycle management and focus on profitable niches.
  • Strategic focus shifted modestly with increased emphasis on technological leadership in AI and data analytics, as well as growth in captives and group captive A&H products.
  • Key financial metrics showed continued improvement: combined ratio improved from 90.5% to 89.4%, non-cat loss ratio improved, and expense ratio declined.
  • Analyst questions in both quarters focused on reserves, growth sustainability, investment volatility, and business mix, with no significant change in underlying concerns.
  • Management's confidence in selective growth and capital reallocation remained firm, with added attention to technological initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified loss inflation as an ongoing headwind, driving selective exposure growth in casualty lines.
  • Investment income volatility from alternative assets was acknowledged, with steps taken to reduce exposure and reinvest in fixed income.
  • Analysts probed the impact of market softening in property, the sustainability of growth in captives, and reserving for volatility-prone lines.
  • No new material reserve development or negative claims trends were reported.

Final Takeaway

Management attributed the quarter's record underwriting income and continued profitable growth to a diversified portfolio, conservative reserving, and disciplined cycle management. The company is strategically reallocating capital to segments with favorable risk-adjusted returns, notably agriculture, credit, and A&H, while leveraging advanced technology and AI to sustain operational efficiency. Confidence in the "Rule Our Niche" strategy was reaffirmed, with the business well positioned to navigate changing market conditions and deliver consistent top-quartile returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Paramount Group raises 2025 core FFO midpoint guidance to $0.57 amid robust leasing and balanced market gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Paramount Group, Inc. (PGRE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Albert Paul Thomas Behler, Chairman, CEO, and President, reported “a strong second quarter with core FFO of $0.17 per share exceeding consensus by $0.03,” attributing this to “robust leasing activity, continued operational discipline and a focused approach to capital allocation.” He emphasized the company’s execution of over 400,000 square feet of leases, the highest quarterly total since 2019, with a balanced split between New York and San Francisco, signaling growing traction in both markets.
  • Behler stated, “As a result of our strong first half performance and the momentum we are seeing across the business, we are raising full year guidance across all key metrics, including core FFO, leasing volume, cash NOI and year-end leased occupancy.” He highlighted strong leasing at flagship assets, record occupancy at 1301 Sixth Avenue, and expansion at 900 Third Avenue.
  • Referring to San Francisco, Behler acknowledged “a period of recalibration” but noted renewed tenant interest, especially from AI, legal, and professional services sectors, with over 190,000 square feet leased in the quarter. He described selective asset sales and joint ventures, specifically referencing the 900 Third Avenue and One Front Street transactions as value-unlocking moves while maintaining operational control.
  • Peter R.C. Brindley, EVP & Head of Real Estate, specified “leasing approximately 405,000 square feet, 52% in New York and the remainder in San Francisco,” and set leasing guidance midpoint at 1.3 million square feet for the year. He described a “robust” pipeline, with over 275,000 square feet in advanced negotiations, and highlighted the impact of AI-based companies in San Francisco’s leasing profile.
  • Ermelinda Berberi, EVP, CFO & Treasurer, stated, “Yesterday afternoon, we reported core FFO of $0.17 per share for the second quarter, exceeding Wall Street consensus estimates by $0.03.” She announced full-year core FFO guidance raised to $0.55–$0.59 per share (midpoint $0.57), leasing guidance at 1.2–1.4 million square feet, and year-end same-store occupancy guidance to 86.9–88.9%.

Outlook

  • The company raised full-year core FFO guidance to a range of $0.55–$0.59 per share, with the midpoint at $0.57 per share, “a $0.03 per share increase from prior guidance at the midpoint,” according to Berberi.
  • Leasing volume guidance was increased to 1.2 million–1.4 million square feet, with a midpoint of 1.3 million square feet, a 30% increase from previous guidance.
  • Same-store lease occupancy guidance was also raised to 86.9%–88.9%, with the midpoint at 87.9%, “a 250 basis point increase from our previous guidance.”
  • Management expects “near-term softness in San Francisco driven by volume of upcoming lease expirations,” but maintains confidence in a long-term recovery due to improving tenant sentiment and early signs of stabilization.

Financial Results

  • Core FFO for the second quarter was $0.17 per share.
  • 14 leases totaling approximately 405,000 square feet were executed with weighted average starting rents of $92 per square foot and an average lease term of 12.9 years.
  • Cash mark-to-market on 205,000 square feet of second-generation space was down 5.4%; GAAP mark-to-market was up 2.6%.
  • Same-store portfolio-wide leased occupancy at share was 85.4%, with New York at 88.1% (up 70 basis points) and San Francisco at 75.1% (down 720 basis points, mainly due to the Google lease expiration at One Market Plaza).
  • Liquidity at quarter-end totaled $534 million in cash and restricted cash, and total debt (excluding noncore assets) was $3.2 billion with no core debt maturities until 2026. A 25% equity interest in One Front Street was sold for $255 million gross asset value, generating $11.5 million in net proceeds.

Q&A

  • Manas Abic, Evercore ISI, asked about tenant demand and leasing activity at 1633 Broadway, specifically the impact of the Showtime Networks move out and current asking rents. Behler responded that “we have active showings,” and Brindley added that “asking rents range from, call it, $70 to $90 per square foot and activity at the moment feels very good.”
  • Abic inquired about leasing concessions, with Brindley stating that “concessions have certainly stabilized we expect net effective rent in New York to increase going forward… In San Francisco TIs are varied… TI as a percentage of initial rent went down in the second quarter.”
  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo, asked about upcoming large move-outs and renovation plans at 1633 Broadway. Brindley replied, “We are making some improvements to improve the ground floor experience at 1633 Broadway… We are active with a number of prospects.”
  • Heck also questioned the potential impact of New York’s mayoral race. Behler replied, “We haven't realized any kind of hesitation with regard to making long-term leases or staying in the city.”
  • Heck further asked if the SEC investigation was affecting the strategic review. Behler stated, “I don't expect that this has any significant impact at all to the strategic review.”
  • William Thomas Catherwood, BTIG, questioned San Francisco leasing demand beyond AI tenants. Behler and Brindley both noted increased activity from legal and finance sectors, with Brindley stating, “We are seeing quite a bit of relocation opportunities, which is really very encouraging.”
  • Catherwood also asked about rent increases in New York. Brindley responded, “We are exercising pricing power in every instance, just about in our Midtown portfolio and starting to push rents.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was inquisitive but generally positive, pressing for detail on leasing pipelines, tenant demand, concessions, and guidance. Questions focused on risk management and operational execution, with no overt skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently highlighting confidence and positive momentum. Brindley and Behler repeatedly referenced strong pipelines, stabilized concessions, and market resilience.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management demonstrated increased optimism, with fewer questions about market uncertainty and more focus on execution and rent growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for core FFO, leasing volume, and occupancy was raised this quarter, compared to reaffirmation and a smaller guidance increase last quarter.
  • Leasing activity increased from 284,000 square feet in Q1 to over 400,000 square feet in Q2.
  • The focus shifted toward balanced leasing momentum in both key markets, with San Francisco’s stabilization cited more frequently as a positive trend.
  • Management’s tone moved from cautious optimism regarding San Francisco to greater confidence in recovery potential, while analysts’ questions centered more on execution and less on downside risks.
  • The company advanced its capital strategy with new asset sales and joint ventures, while continuing to emphasize balance sheet strength and flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged “near-term softness in San Francisco driven by volume of upcoming lease expirations.”
  • The scheduled move-outs, particularly Google in San Francisco and Showtime in New York, remain a focus, with mitigation efforts including active leasing and enhanced amenities.
  • Brindley noted, “We are making some improvements to improve the ground floor experience at 1633 Broadway,” and cited ongoing negotiations for large vacant spaces.
  • The SEC inquiry was addressed, with Behler stating it is not expected to impact the ongoing strategic review.

Final Takeaway

Paramount Group management highlighted strong leasing momentum, rising occupancy, and improved financial guidance, underpinned by robust demand for high-quality office space in both New York and San Francisco. The company’s strategic capital allocation and operational discipline position it to navigate ongoing market shifts, with a focus on value creation through leasing, asset management, and selective partnerships. Management remains confident in the long-term outlook for its core markets and committed to maintaining flexibility and balance sheet strength as it pursues further growth and shareholder value maximization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AptarGroup beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; initiates Q3 outlook
data.description:
• AptarGroup press release (NYSE:ATR): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.66 beats by $0.07.
  • Revenue of $966.01M (+6.1% Y/Y) beats by $9.15M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased 13% from the prior year to $218 million.
  • Aptar currently expects earnings per share for the third quarter of 2025, excluding any restructuring expenses, changes in the fair value of equity investments and acquisition costs, to be in the range of $1.53 to $1.61 (vs. consensus of $1.65), which includes approximately 6 to 7 cents of higher legal fees associated with litigating pharma intellectual property rights. This guidance is based on an effective tax rate range of 20.5% to 22.5%, primarily due to a one-time tax benefit, with a comparable adjusted prior year effective tax rate of 23.8%. The earnings per share guidance range is based on current spot rates and a 1.15 Euro to USD exchange rate.

data.title: Gerdau GAAP EPS of R$0.43, revenue of R$17.53B
data.description:
• Gerdau press release (NYSE:GGB): Q2 GAAP EPS of R$0.43.
  • Revenue of R$17.53B (+5.5% Y/Y).
  • Steel shipments of 2.8 million tonnes in 2Q25, in line with 1Q25.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of R$2.6 billion in 2Q25, up 7% from 1Q25.
  • Investments (CAPEX) of R$1.6 billion in 2Q25, of which 80% was earmarked for Brazil.
  • Based on 2Q25 results, the Company approved the distribution of dividends in the amount of R$0.12 per share, totaling R$239.5 million. In addition, we are consistently executing our share buyback program, approved on January 20, 2025, in which we have already repurchased 43.8 million shares (GGBR3, GGBR4 and/or GGB), approximately 68% of the total program and 2.2% of the Company's outstanding shares.

data.title: Vanguard California Tax-Exempt Bond ETF declares monthly distribution of $0.2435
data.description:
• Vanguard California Tax-Exempt Bond ETF (BATS:VTEC) - $0.2435.
  • 30-Day SEC Yield of 3.57% as of July 30.
  • Payable Aug 05; for shareholders of record Aug 01; ex-div Aug 01.
